stem: An OTA is treating a client with a Zone V extensor tendon repair. Which splinting position is indicated?
options: Wrist 30-40° extension, MCPs 0° (neutral), IPs free — to protect the repaired extensor tendon over the dorsal hand
rationale: Zone V extensor repair splinting: wrist 30-40° extension, MCPs 0° (prevents tension on the repair), IPs free. Early controlled motion protocols vary; follow the surgeon's specific protocol.

stem: A client with bilateral upper extremity weakness due to myasthenia gravis has fluctuating strength throughout the day. When should the OTA schedule functional activities?
options: End of the day when muscles are warmed up
Morning or after rest periods when the client's strength is typically best — myasthenia gravis causes fatigable weakness
rationale: Myasthenia gravis: fatigable weakness that worsens with activity and improves with rest. Schedule important functional activities in the morning or after rest when strength is greatest.

stem: An OTA is working with a client who has a C6 spinal cord injury and is learning to use a tenodesis grasp. Which wrist movement activates the grasp?
options: Active wrist extension passively tensions the finger flexor tendons, producing finger flexion (grasp)
rationale: Tenodesis grasp (C6 SCI): wrist extension → passive finger flexion (grasp). Wrist flexion → passive finger extension (release). The OTA should avoid stretching finger flexors to preserve this mechanism.

stem: A client with a recent diagnosis of amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) asks about assistive technology. What is the OTA's role?
options: Wait until all function is lost
Proactively introduce and train assistive technology while the client can still learn; plan for progressive needs
Refer to a different discipline entirely
rationale: ALS AT: proactive introduction while learning capacity exists. Progress from low-tech to high-tech as function declines. Plan for voice banking, eye-gaze technology, and environmental control units.

stem: An OTA is educating a client about the proper use of a reacher. Which technique is correct?
options: Use it to reach heavy objects overhead
Use it for lightweight objects; avoid twisting the wrist; maintain good posture during reaching; and never use it to lift items heavier than 5 pounds
Extend the arm fully and lean
Use it as a walking aid
rationale: Reacher use: lightweight items only (typically <5 lbs), maintain upright posture, avoid twisting, squeeze and release smoothly, and avoid overhead reaching for heavy or fragile items.

stem: A client with severe rheumatoid arthritis has MCP joint subluxation and ulnar drift. Which splint addresses this deformity?
options: Ulnar drift splint or MCP alignment splint that provides a radial pull to counteract the ulnar deviating forces
rationale: RA ulnar drift splinting: ulnar drift positioning splint provides radial deviation force at the MCPs to counteract ulnar drift. May include MCP extension block. Used during activities or at rest.

stem: An OTA is documenting a client's FIM score for grooming. The client completes grooming independently but requires twice the normal time. What FIM score is appropriate?
options: FIM 6 (Modified Independence) — the client is independent but requires more time than normal
rationale: FIM 6 (Modified Independence): the client performs the activity independently but requires additional time, adaptive equipment, or safety considerations. No helper is needed.

stem: When documenting objective measurements, the OTA should include which goniometric data?
options: Both active and passive ROM for the affected and unaffected sides, the joint measured, the motion tested, and comparison to normative values
rationale: Goniometric documentation: specific joint, motion tested, AROM and PROM values, bilateral comparison, normative reference, pain during measurement, and end-feel if applicable.

stem: An OTA is treating a client who becomes dizzy during an activity. What vital sign should be checked first?
options: Blood pressure — dizziness may indicate orthostatic hypotension, hypertension, or cardiac arrhythmia
rationale: Dizziness during activity: check blood pressure first (orthostatic hypotension is common, especially with position changes). Also assess heart rate, oxygen saturation, and blood glucose if diabetic.

stem: A client with a chest tube is participating in OT bedside activities. What precaution is essential?
options: Keep the collection chamber below the level of the chest insertion site, avoid tension on the tube, and monitor for respiratory distress
rationale: Chest tube precautions: collection below insertion site, avoid pulling/kinking the tube, monitor respiratory status, avoid disconnecting the system, and keep tubing secure during activity.

stem: An OTA is leading a cooking group for clients with mental health conditions. What therapeutic factor does this group address?
options: Social participation, task performance, self-efficacy, sequencing skills, following directions, and life skills for community living
rationale: Cooking group therapeutic factors: life skills development, social participation, task sequencing, following directions, self-efficacy, problem-solving, sensory experiences, and community living preparation.

stem: A client with hemiplegia is learning one-handed shoe tying. Which technique is MOST commonly taught?
options: One-handed shoe tying technique using a loop-and-wrap method or elastic shoelaces to eliminate the need for tying
Ask someone else to tie shoes
rationale: One-handed shoe tying options: one-handed bow technique, elastic no-tie laces, coil laces, Velcro closure shoes, or slip-on shoes. Choose based on client preference, cognition, and hand dexterity.

stem: An OTA is treating a child with sensory modulation disorder who is over-responsive to tactile input. Which intervention approach is used?
options: Gradual, systematic desensitization starting with preferred textures and slowly introducing new ones within a safe, supportive environment
rationale: Tactile over-responsivity: gradual desensitization (start with preferred textures), predictable input (firm touch over light touch), deep pressure before tactile activities, and safe supportive environment.

stem: A 5-year-old child has difficulty with handwriting and demonstrates poor pencil grasp. Which grasp pattern is age-appropriate?
options: Dynamic tripod grasp — thumb, index, and middle finger control the pencil with wrist stabilization and finger movement
rationale: Dynamic tripod grasp (by age 4.5-6): thumb, index, and middle finger control the pencil with movement from the fingers (not the wrist or arm). Considered the mature functional grasp pattern.

stem: An OTA observes a client with cognitive impairment attempting to eat soup with a fork. This demonstrates which cognitive deficit?
options: Object agnosia or impaired tool-use knowledge — inability to match the correct tool to the task
rationale: Using the wrong tool for a task can indicate ideational apraxia (impaired tool-use knowledge/sequencing) or object agnosia (inability to recognize the purpose of objects). Provide correct tools with cueing.

stem: A client with a median nerve injury at the wrist demonstrates which hand deformity?
options: Ape hand deformity — loss of thenar muscle function causing the thumb to fall into the plane of the palm, unable to oppose
rationale: Median nerve at wrist: ape hand (thenar atrophy, loss of opposition, thumb falls into palm plane). Also lose sensation in the lateral 3.5 digits. Cannot make a precision pinch.

stem: An OTA is addressing community reintegration with a client who has had a stroke. Which activity represents the highest level of community participation?
options: Independently managing a volunteer job or returning to paid employment with or without accommodations
rationale: Community reintegration hierarchy: self-care → home management → community errands → social participation → volunteer/work. Work or volunteer participation represents the highest level of community participation.

stem: A client with chronic pain reports a pain level of 4/10 at rest and 7/10 with activity. Using the biopsychosocial model, what should the OTA address?
options: Physical (body mechanics, pacing), psychological (catastrophizing, self-efficacy), and social (role performance, social participation) factors
rationale: Biopsychosocial approach: physical (exercise, ergonomics, modalities), psychological (cognitive restructuring, relaxation, self-efficacy), and social (role performance, social participation, return to work).

stem: An OTA is working on caregiver training before a client with dementia is discharged home. What education is MOST important?
options: Safety strategies, consistent daily routine, communication techniques, self-care maintenance techniques, and caregiver self-care/burnout prevention
rationale: Dementia caregiver training: safety strategies, consistent routine, effective communication, ADL assistance techniques, environmental modification, behavioral management, and caregiver wellness/respite resources.

stem: A geriatric client with osteoporosis asks about calcium and vitamin D supplementation. What is the OTA's appropriate response?
options: Explain that nutrition advice is best directed to their physician or dietitian; the OTA can educate about weight-bearing exercise and fall prevention for bone health
rationale: Supplement recommendations are outside OT scope. Refer to physician/dietitian. OTA can educate on weight-bearing exercise (builds bone), fall prevention (reduces fracture risk), and posture.

stem: An OTA is treating a client with an anxiety disorder who becomes panicked during a therapy session. What is the FIRST intervention?
options: Guide the client through deep breathing exercises, reduce environmental stimulation, and provide reassurance in a calm, supportive manner
rationale: Anxiety/panic response: remain calm, guide deep breathing (4-7-8 pattern), reduce environmental stimulation (noise, lights, people), provide reassurance, grounding techniques, and modify the activity.

stem: A client with a new above-elbow amputation is being prepared for prosthetic training. What pre-prosthetic activities does the OTA address?
options: Residual limb care and shaping, desensitization, ROM maintenance, strengthening, dominance retraining if applicable, and one-handed technique training
rationale: Pre-prosthetic OT: residual limb care (wrapping, shaping, desensitization), ROM/strengthening, one-handed ADL techniques, dominance retraining, phantom limb management, and psychological adjustment.

stem: An OTA is working with a client who has low vision due to macular degeneration. Which adaptation is MOST helpful for reading?
options: Magnification devices (handheld or desktop magnifier), large-print materials, high-contrast text, and adequate task lighting
rationale: Macular degeneration reading adaptations: magnification devices, large-print materials, high contrast (black on white/yellow), task lighting, reading stands, CCTV magnifiers, and eccentric viewing training.

stem: A client with a recent total hip replacement is in the hospital and needs to use the toilet. What adaptive equipment is necessary?
options: Raised toilet seat (increases seat height to avoid >90° hip flexion) with armrests for support during sit-to-stand transfers
Bedside commode placed over the toilet only
rationale: Post-THR toilet: raised toilet seat (prevents >90° hip flexion), armrests (assist with transfers), follow hip precautions during transfers, and use grab bars if available.

stem: An OTA is treating a client who has developed heterotopic ossification (HO) around the elbow after a burn. What is the rehabilitation approach?
options: Gentle AROM within pain-free range, functional activities, and avoid forceful PROM which can worsen the HO formation
rationale: HO rehabilitation: gentle AROM within comfortable range, functional activities, avoid forceful PROM (can worsen HO). May need positioning and dynamic splinting. Monitor ROM changes.

stem: A client with bilateral hand arthritis has difficulty opening medication bottles. Which adaptation is MOST effective?
options: Request non-child-resistant caps from the pharmacy and use a weekly pill organizer prepared by a caregiver or pharmacist
rationale: Medication bottle adaptations: non-child-resistant caps (request from pharmacy), weekly pill organizers (can be pre-filled), automatic pill dispensers, and lever-style bottle openers.

stem: An OTA is assessing a client's ability to manage finances. Which cognitive skills are required for bill paying?
options: Sequencing, mathematical calculation, attention to detail, organization, planning, memory, and judgment about financial priorities
rationale: Financial management cognitive demands: sequencing, calculation, attention, organization, planning, memory (due dates), judgment (priorities), and problem-solving (managing discrepancies).

stem: A client with a traumatic brain injury demonstrates impulsivity and poor safety awareness. What environmental modification helps?
options: Structured, uncluttered environment with clear boundaries, locked exits (if needed for safety), and removal of hazardous items
rationale: TBI impulsivity/safety: structured environment, reduced stimulation, clear boundaries, locked exits if needed, hazard removal, consistent routines, and 1:1 supervision with gradual independence.

stem: When the OTA is treating a client with a new colostomy, what body image concern should be addressed?
options: Adjustment to altered body appearance and function, clothing adaptations, intimacy concerns, and social participation anxiety
Only skin care around the stoma
rationale: Colostomy body image: adjustment to altered appearance/function, clothing adaptations (looser clothing, special undergarments), intimacy discussions, social participation (noise/odor concerns), and peer support.

stem: An OTA is planning a leisure activity program for clients in a psychiatric rehabilitation setting. What principle guides activity selection?
options: Select activities based on client interests, functional abilities, therapeutic goals, and potential for carryover to community settings
rationale: Leisure activity selection: based on client interests/preferences, appropriate to functional level, addresses therapeutic goals, promotes social interaction, and can be continued in community settings.

stem: A client with a brachial plexus injury affecting C5-C6 (Erb's palsy) has which functional limitation?
options: Inability to flex the elbow, supinate the forearm, and abduct/externally rotate the shoulder (waiter's tip position)
rationale: Erb's palsy (C5-C6): loss of shoulder abduction/ER, elbow flexion, forearm supination. Waiter's tip position. Intact wrist/hand function. Functional impact: cannot reach to face for feeding/grooming.

stem: An OTA is treating a client with scleroderma affecting the hands. Which treatment is MOST important?
options: Active ROM exercises, skin care and hydration, joint protection, and paraffin/heat to maintain hand flexibility and prevent contractures
rationale: Scleroderma hand treatment: AROM exercises (maintain flexibility), skin care/hydration, paraffin wax (increase extensibility), joint protection, and adaptive equipment for functional limitations.

stem: A client with a peripheral vestibular disorder feels dizzy when turning in bed. Which vestibular rehabilitation exercise addresses this?
options: Habituation exercises: repeated controlled exposure to the provocative movement (turning in bed) to reduce dizziness response over time
rationale: Vestibular habituation: repeated controlled exposure to the motion that provokes dizziness. Over time, the CNS adapts and the dizziness response decreases. Performed under OT/PT guidance.

stem: An OTA is working with a client who has executive dysfunction after a frontal lobe injury. Which strategy helps with meal planning?
options: Written checklists, recipe cards with step-by-step instructions, pre-planning meals on a weekly calendar, and external organizational aids
Let the client figure it out independently
rationale: Executive dysfunction meal planning: written checklists, step-by-step recipe cards, weekly meal calendar, grocery list templates, organizational aids, and gradual fading of supports as skills improve.

stem: A client who had a stroke 3 weeks ago has shoulder subluxation and is developing shoulder-hand syndrome (CRPS). Which early intervention is critical?
options: Aggressive edema management (elevation, retrograde massage, compression), gentle AROM, proper positioning, and pain management to prevent progression
rationale: Shoulder-hand syndrome early intervention: edema management, gentle AROM (avoid pain), proper positioning (arm supported), pain management, desensitization, and stress loading program (scrubbing, weight-bearing).

stem: An OTA is treating a child with autism who resists tooth brushing. Which sensory-based approach helps?
options: Use a vibrating toothbrush (provides proprioceptive input), desensitize the mouth area gradually, use visual schedules, and let the child choose flavored toothpaste
rationale: Autism oral hygiene: vibrating toothbrush (proprioceptive input), oral desensitization (gradual introduction), visual schedule, choice of flavored toothpaste, consistent routine, and social stories.

stem: A client with a complete C5 SCI wants to feed independently. What adaptive equipment setup is needed?
options: Mobile arm support (MAS), universal cuff with built-up utensil, plate guard, non-slip mat, and gravity-eliminated forearm position
No equipment — client cannot feed at C5
rationale: C5 SCI feeding: MAS (supports arm against gravity), universal cuff (utensil holder), built-up handle, plate guard, non-slip mat, and adapted cup with long straw. Can achieve modified independence.

stem: An OTA is providing hand therapy for a client with trigger finger. After a cortisone injection, what does the OTA educate the client about?
options: Avoid heavy gripping for 24-48 hours, may have temporary pain increase, report signs of infection, and continue gentle tendon gliding exercises
rationale: Post-cortisone injection education: avoid heavy use 24-48 hours, temporary pain flare possible, report infection signs (redness, warmth, drainage, fever), continue gentle tendon gliding, and follow up.

stem: A client with bilateral hip replacements is being instructed in lower body dressing. What technique does the OTA teach?
options: Use a reacher to position pants, dressing stick for pulling up, sock aid for donning socks, long shoe horn, and elastic shoelaces — all while maintaining hip precautions
Stand on one leg to dress
rationale: Bilateral THR dressing: reacher, dressing stick, sock aid, long shoe horn, elastic laces, dress seated, avoid >90° hip flexion, avoid crossing legs, and use adaptive techniques for both sides.

stem: An OTA notices that a client's burn scar is hypertrophic and raised. Which scar management intervention is indicated?
options: Pressure garments (worn 23 hours/day), silicone gel sheets, scar massage, and moisturizing to manage the hypertrophic scar
rationale: Hypertrophic burn scar management: pressure garments (23 hrs/day for up to 2 years), silicone gel sheets, scar massage, moisturizing, and positioning/splinting to prevent contracture.

stem: A client with a low vision diagnosis needs to identify medications. Which strategy helps?
options: Use tactile markers on different bottles, large-print labels, talking prescription readers, weekly pill organizer, and pharmacy-provided large labels
rationale: Low vision medication identification: tactile markers (rubber bands, Braille dots), large-print labels, talking prescription readers, weekly organizer, color-coded systems, and pharmacy assistance.

stem: An OTA is performing a home safety assessment. Which environmental hazard is MOST commonly overlooked?
options: Inadequate lighting — dim hallways, stairwells, and bathrooms significantly increase fall risk, especially for older adults
rationale: Inadequate lighting: commonly overlooked hazard. Recommendations: nightlights in hallways/bathrooms, brighter bulbs, light switches accessible at doorways, motion-sensor lights, and stairway lighting.

stem: A client with depression and low motivation is refusing to participate in OT. What approach does the OTA use?
options: Offer a meaningful, achievable activity choice, acknowledge feelings without judgment, use motivational interviewing, and break tasks into small steps
rationale: Depression and low motivation: offer choices (autonomy), start with preferred activities (success experience), acknowledge feelings, motivational interviewing, small achievable steps, and consistent rapport.

stem: An OTA is treating a client with complex regional pain syndrome (CRPS) Type I of the hand. Which intervention is contraindicated?
options: Forceful passive stretching or aggressive mobilization — this can exacerbate pain and worsen the condition
rationale: CRPS contraindication: forceful PROM/aggressive mobilization can worsen pain and sympathetic nervous system response. Use gentle AROM, stress loading, desensitization, mirror therapy, and graded motor imagery.

stem: A client post-stroke is using a lap tray on their wheelchair. What purpose does this serve for the hemiplegic arm?
options: Supports the affected arm in a visible position, prevents shoulder subluxation in sitting, provides proprioceptive input, and enables bilateral activities
rationale: Lap tray therapeutic purposes: supports the affected arm (reduces subluxation), visual awareness of the arm, proprioceptive input, positioning for bilateral activities, and prevents dependent edema.

stem: An OTA is conducting a kitchen safety assessment with a client who has cognitive impairments. What is the MOST critical safety concern?
options: Fire safety — stove use, unattended cooking, flammable materials near heat sources, and the client's ability to respond to a fire or smoke alarm
rationale: Kitchen fire safety for cognitive impairment: stove safety (auto shut-off, supervised use only), no unattended cooking, smoke detectors, fire extinguisher, clear flammable items, and emergency response ability.

stem: A client with bilateral below-knee amputations is learning wheelchair management. What skill is essential for community mobility?
options: Navigating curbs, ramps, uneven surfaces, doorways, and transfers in and out of a vehicle — community wheelchair skills training
rationale: Community wheelchair skills: curb negotiation, ramp management, uneven terrain, doorway navigation, vehicle transfers, elevator use, and public transportation access.

stem: An OTA is writing a progress note and the client showed no measurable change since the last session. How should this be documented?
options: Document accurately: describe what was attempted, the client's performance level, possible reasons for plateau, and the plan to modify treatment or discuss with the OT
rationale: No-change documentation: honest reporting, describe treatment attempted, performance level, possible plateau reasons (medical, motivational, environmental), and plan (modify treatment, consult OT, consider discharge).

stem: A client is being seen for outpatient hand therapy. Insurance requires prior authorization for continued treatment. What documentation supports the authorization?
options: Measurable functional progress, remaining deficits, skilled intervention necessity, rehabilitation potential, and specific functional goals with timelines
rationale: Prior authorization documentation: objective progress data, remaining functional deficits, why skilled OT is still needed, rehabilitation potential, specific measurable goals, and estimated treatment timeline.

stem: An OTA is treating a client with shoulder adhesive capsulitis. What is the capsular pattern of restriction at the glenohumeral joint?
options: External rotation most limited, followed by abduction, then internal rotation
rationale: Glenohumeral capsular pattern: ER most limited > abduction > IR. This pattern distinguishes capsular restriction from non-capsular (e.g., subacromial impingement limits active but not passive motion).

stem: A client with a peripheral nerve injury is demonstrating Tinel's sign at the wrist. What does this indicate?
options: Nerve regeneration — tapping over the nerve produces tingling/electrical sensation at the most distal point of regeneration
rationale: Tinel's sign: tapping over a nerve produces tingling at the most distal point of regeneration. The advancing Tinel's sign tracks nerve recovery. Also used diagnostically for nerve compression (CTS).

stem: An OTA is educating a client about the proper way to carry groceries. Which technique protects the joints?
options: Carry all bags in one hand
Use reusable bags with padded handles, distribute weight evenly between both hands, carry bags on forearms rather than in fingers, and use a cart when possible
Carry as much as possible in one trip
rationale: Joint protection for carrying: distribute weight evenly, carry on forearms (not fingers), use padded handles, take multiple trips, use a cart/wagon, and avoid sustained heavy gripping.

stem: A geriatric client with severe kyphosis has difficulty with overhead reaching. Which home modification helps?
options: Lower shelving, pull-down shelf organizers, reacher for upper shelves, and reorganize frequently used items within easy reach
rationale: Kyphosis overhead adaptations: lower shelving/pegboard systems, pull-down shelf organizers, reacher for higher items, reorganize (frequently used items at waist-shoulder level), and long-handled tools.

stem: An OTA is working with a client who has a traumatic hand injury and demonstrates fear-avoidance behavior. What approach addresses this?
options: Force the client to use the hand
Graded exposure therapy: gradually increasing hand use in functional activities while addressing pain beliefs and building self-efficacy through achievable goals
rationale: Fear-avoidance in hand rehab: graded exposure, pain neuroscience education, achievable functional goals (builds self-efficacy), cognitive restructuring of pain beliefs, and systematic desensitization.

stem: A client with a C7 SCI is learning to perform a depression transfer. Which muscle makes this possible?
options: Triceps — C7 SCI preserves triceps function, allowing the client to push up (depress) their body weight through locked elbows for transfers
rationale: Depression transfer (C7 SCI): triceps (elbow extension) allows push-up through locked elbows, lifting the body for sliding board or pivot transfers. Combined with shoulder depressors for full lift.

stem: An OTA is fabricating a custom thermoplastic splint. What is the typical water temperature for softening the material?
options: 150-170°F (65-77°C) — heat the thermoplastic until uniformly soft and conformable, then mold to the client's extremity
rationale: Thermoplastic splinting: heat in 150-170°F water until uniformly soft, dry excess water, form to the extremity, hold until set, and adjust fit. Different materials have different temperature requirements.

stem: A client with hemiplegia demonstrates learned nonuse of the affected upper extremity. What does this mean?
options: The client has stopped trying to use the affected arm due to repeated failure, even though some motor recovery exists — the brain has 'forgotten' the arm
rationale: Learned nonuse: after repeated failure, the client stops attempting to use the affected arm (even with motor recovery). The brain's cortical representation shrinks. CIMT can reverse this process.

stem: An OTA is treating a client in a burn rehabilitation unit. Which exercise timing relative to wound care is MOST effective?
options: After wound care when the tissue is warm and more extensible, and pain medication has taken effect — ROM exercises are more effective
Exercise is not done on burn patients
rationale: Burn exercise timing: after wound care (tissue warm, cleaned, medicated). Warmed tissue is more extensible for ROM exercises. Coordinate with pain medication schedule for optimal participation.

stem: A client with early-stage Alzheimer's disease needs help maintaining independence in medication management. Which compensatory strategy is appropriate?
options: Automated pill dispenser with alarms, simplified medication schedule (once or twice daily), and caregiver backup for monitoring
No strategies — the client should manage independently
rationale: Early Alzheimer's medication management: automated dispensers, simplified schedule, visual cues, medication calendar, caregiver monitoring/backup, and pharmacist consultation for simplification.

stem: When an OTA is treating a client with bilateral upper extremity involvement and the client needs to sign legal documents, what adaptation can be used?
options: Adapted pen holder, mouth stick for writing, electronic signature pad, voice-to-text for document preparation, or designated power of attorney
rationale: Signature adaptations: built-up pen, universal cuff with pen, mouth stick, electronic signature, stamped signature (legal in many states with witness), or power of attorney for legal documents.

stem: An OTA is treating a client with post-concussion syndrome. Which environmental modification helps reduce symptoms during therapy?
options: Quiet, dimly lit room with minimal visual stimulation, reduced screen time, and controlled pacing of cognitive demands
rationale: Post-concussion environment: reduced lighting, quiet space, minimal visual stimulation, controlled cognitive pacing, rest breaks, and gradual increase in environmental complexity as symptoms improve.

stem: A client with Ehlers-Danlos syndrome (hypermobility type) has chronic joint instability. What is the OTA's treatment focus?
options: Joint stabilization through strengthening, proprioceptive training, joint protection education, and activity modification to prevent subluxation
rationale: EDS hypermobility OT: strengthening (joint stabilization), proprioceptive training, joint protection education, pacing, splinting (stabilize lax joints), and activity modification to prevent subluxation.

stem: An OTA notices a colleague documenting services not actually provided. What is the ethical obligation?
options: Report the fraudulent documentation to the supervisor and follow facility and professional ethics reporting procedures
Help the colleague cover it up
rationale: Fraudulent documentation: ethical and legal obligation to report. Follow facility reporting procedures, report to the supervisor, and if not resolved, escalate to the state licensing board or AOTA ethics commission.

stem: A client with a spinal cord injury is at risk for pressure injuries. What is the OTA's role in pressure injury prevention?
options: Teach weight-shifting techniques (every 15-30 minutes), proper wheelchair cushion use, skin inspection, and nutrition education for skin health
No OT role in skin care
rationale: OT pressure injury prevention: weight-shifting education (every 15-30 minutes), wheelchair cushion selection, skin inspection routine, positioning, nutrition education, and adaptive equipment for independence.

stem: When an OTA is treating a client with a new tracheostomy, what safety awareness is critical?
options: Maintain humidification, suction equipment at bedside, avoid water near the tracheostomy site, and ensure the emergency call system is within reach
rationale: Tracheostomy safety: humidification, suction equipment nearby, protect from water, emergency call within reach, and communicate with nursing about any changes in respiratory status during therapy.

stem: An OTA is leading a social skills group for adolescents with developmental disabilities. Which activity promotes turn-taking and reciprocal communication?
options: Cooperative board games where players must communicate, wait their turn, follow rules, and work together toward a shared goal
rationale: Social skills group activities: cooperative games (turn-taking, communication), role-playing social scenarios, group art projects, cooking together, and structured social activities with feedback.

stem: A client with a recent amputation is experiencing phantom limb pain. Which OT intervention may help?
options: Mirror therapy (visual feedback of the intact limb creates an illusion of the amputated limb), desensitization, and graded motor imagery
rationale: Phantom limb pain OT: mirror therapy (visual cortex feedback), graded motor imagery, desensitization, TENS, relaxation techniques, and functional use of the residual limb.

stem: An OTA is conducting a wheelchair seating evaluation. What measurement determines the correct seat depth?
options: Measure from the posterior buttock to the popliteal crease minus 1-2 inches to prevent pressure on the back of the knee
rationale: Wheelchair seat depth: measure from the posterior buttock to the popliteal crease minus 1-2 inches. Too deep = popliteal pressure (DVT risk). Too shallow = insufficient thigh support.

stem: When is it appropriate for the OTA to discharge a client from OT services?
options: When goals are met, no further progress is expected, the client refuses treatment, or per the OT's direction — the OT makes the final discharge decision
After a set number of visits regardless
rationale: OT discharge criteria: goals met, progress plateau (no further skilled OT benefit), client refusal, safety concerns, or OT/physician direction. The supervising OT makes the final discharge decision.

stem: A client with a colostomy needs to learn to change the pouch independently. What is the correct sequence?
options: Empty and remove the old pouch, clean and dry the peristomal skin, measure and cut the new wafer, apply skin barrier, press the new wafer firmly, and attach the pouch
Apply the new pouch over the old one
rationale: Colostomy pouch change: empty and remove old pouch, clean peristomal skin (gentle soap and water), dry thoroughly, apply skin barrier, measure/cut wafer, apply wafer firmly, and attach new pouch.

stem: An OTA is treating a client who is post-mastectomy and has developed lymphedema in the affected arm. Which daily precaution should the client follow?
options: Avoid blood draws, BP measurements, and injections in the affected arm; wear compression garment; avoid extreme temperatures; and protect skin from cuts/burns
rationale: Post-mastectomy lymphedema precautions: no BP/blood draws/injections in the affected arm, compression garment, skin protection, avoid extreme heat, gentle exercise, and elevation when resting.

stem: A client with cognitive deficits after TBI is learning to use a memory notebook. What sections should be included?
options: Personal information, daily schedule, memory log, to-do lists, important contacts, therapy notes, and orientation information
rationale: Memory notebook sections: orientation (date, location, situation), daily schedule, to-do lists, memory log (important events), contacts, therapy goals/notes, and transportation information.

stem: An OTA is treating a child with cerebral palsy who has difficulty with oral motor control during feeding. Which positioning supports improved feeding?
options: Upright seated position with hips at 90°, feet supported, head in midline with slight chin tuck to protect the airway
rationale: CP feeding position: upright (90° hips), feet flat/supported, trunk support as needed, head in midline, slight chin tuck (reduces aspiration risk), and stable symmetrical posture.

stem: A client with rheumatoid arthritis asks about joint protection during household cleaning. What technique should the OTA teach?
options: Use lightweight tools with extended handles, alternate between tasks, avoid sustained gripping, use both hands to distribute forces, and rest frequently
rationale: RA joint protection for cleaning: lightweight tools, extended handles, alternate tasks, avoid sustained grip, use both hands, pace with rest breaks, ergonomic tools, and energy conservation.

stem: An OTA is working with a client who has bilateral shoulder replacements and cannot reach behind their back. How should the OTA address perineal hygiene?
options: Long-handled wiping aid (toilet tissue aid), bidet attachment, and training in safe techniques that avoid shoulder precaution violations
rationale: Bilateral shoulder replacement perineal hygiene: long-handled wiping aid, bidet attachment (reduces reaching), adaptive techniques within shoulder precautions, and one-handed techniques if needed.

stem: A client with multiple sclerosis is experiencing heat-induced symptom worsening (Uhthoff's phenomenon). What environmental modifications does the OTA recommend?
options: Air conditioning, cooling vest during activities, tepid (not hot) showers, avoid midday outdoor activities, and pre-cooling before exercise
rationale: MS heat management: air conditioning, cooling vest/neck wrap, tepid showers, avoid heat exposure (midday, hot environments), pre-cool before exercise, and recognize early symptoms of overheating.

stem: An OTA is assessing a client's executive function using a real-world task. Which activity BEST tests planning, sequencing, and problem-solving?
options: Preparing a multi-step meal that requires timing, ingredient gathering, sequencing steps, and managing multiple components simultaneously
rationale: Meal preparation tests multiple executive functions: planning, sequencing, time management, multi-tasking, problem-solving, safety awareness, and working memory. Highly ecological valid assessment.

stem: A client with a wound VAC (negative pressure wound therapy) device is participating in OT. What precaution applies?
options: Ensure the device is functioning (not alarming), avoid disconnecting the tubing, protect the wound site during activities, and coordinate with nursing for any issues
Turn off the device during therapy
rationale: Wound VAC precautions: keep the device running, avoid tubing disconnection/kinking, protect the wound site, avoid placing pressure on the wound, and contact nursing if the device alarms.

stem: An OTA is teaching a client with hemiplegia to manage fasteners one-handed. Which adaptive technique is taught for buttons?
options: Sew buttons on with elastic thread (allows pulling garment on/off without unbuttoning) or use a button hook device
rationale: One-handed button management: elastic thread (buttons stay closed, stretch to pull on/off), button hook (hooks through buttonhole, grasps button), or button aids. Maintain independence in dressing.

stem: A client who is deaf and uses American Sign Language (ASL) needs OT services. What communication approach should the OTA use?
options: Request a certified ASL interpreter, face the client directly, ensure adequate lighting, and learn basic signs for common therapy terms
rationale: Deaf client communication: certified ASL interpreter for clinical communication, face the client directly (visual cues), adequate lighting, learn basic signs, visual demonstrations, and written supplements.

stem: An OTA is treating a client with fibromyalgia. Which exercise intensity is recommended?
options: Low-to-moderate intensity aerobic exercise (walking, aquatic therapy) starting below the client's pain threshold and progressing gradually
rationale: Fibromyalgia exercise: low-to-moderate intensity, start below pain threshold, aerobic (walking, aquatics), gradual progression, pacing, and avoid pushing through significant pain increases.

stem: A client with an ileostomy is concerned about returning to swimming. What does the OTA advise?
options: Swimming is possible with a secure pouch, waterproof tape over the edges, and changing the pouch after swimming — timing with meals helps
rationale: Ostomy swimming: secure pouch attachment, waterproof tape over edges, change pouch after swimming, time swimming between meals (less output), mini pouch or stoma cap available for swimming.

stem: An OTA is working with a team on a seating clinic for a client who uses a power wheelchair. What factors influence power wheelchair prescription?
options: Client's cognitive/perceptual status, motor control capabilities, home/community environments, transportation needs, and positioning requirements
rationale: Power wheelchair prescription factors: cognitive/perceptual status (safe driving), motor control (input device selection), environment (home, community, vehicle), positioning needs, and functional goals.

stem: A client with early-stage Parkinson's disease has a resting tremor that interferes with writing. Which adaptation helps?
options: Write faster to outpace the tremor
Use a weighted pen, anchor the forearm on the writing surface, use lined paper, and consider electronic alternatives (tablet, voice-to-text)
rationale: PD resting tremor writing adaptations: weighted pen (dampens tremor), anchor forearm on the table, lined/raised-line paper, larger writing surface, and electronic alternatives (tablet, voice-to-text).

stem: An OTA is working with a client who has bilateral hand burns. What is the anti-deformity position for the thumb?
options: Thumb in palmar abduction (away from the palm) with MCP and IP slightly flexed — opposite of the typical burn contracture pattern
Thumb in opposition to the small finger
rationale: Burn thumb anti-deformity: palmar abduction (maintains first web space), MCP and IP in slight flexion. Burns tend to contract the thumb web space; positioning prevents adduction contracture.

stem: A client with a spinal cord injury at T10 has full upper extremity function. What level of ADL independence is expected?
options: Independent in all self-care, transfers, and wheelchair mobility; may need adaptive equipment for lower body dressing from wheelchair level
rationale: T10 SCI: full UE function, trunk stability (partial abdominals). Independent in all ADLs, transfers, wheelchair mobility. May use adaptive techniques for lower body dressing (long-handled tools).

stem: An OTA is providing discharge planning for a client with a new power wheelchair. What home modifications are typically needed?
options: Wider doorways (if <32"), ramp at entrance, accessible bathroom (roll-in shower), charging station location, and clear pathways for wheelchair navigation
rationale: Power wheelchair home modifications: doorway widening (32-36"), ramp (1:12 slope), accessible bathroom, charging station (near electrical outlet), flooring changes (low-pile carpet), and furniture rearrangement.

stem: A geriatric client demonstrates decreased reaction time and processing speed. How does this affect driving safety?
options: Increased risk of accidents at intersections, difficulty merging, delayed response to unexpected events, and difficulty with complex traffic situations
rationale: Decreased reaction time + driving: increased accident risk (intersections, merging), delayed response to unexpected events, difficulty with complex traffic, and need for driving evaluation/rehabilitation.

stem: An OTA is working with a client who has a feeding tube (PEG tube). Can the client participate in oral motor activities?
options: Never — feeding tubes mean no oral activities
Yes, if cleared by the physician and SLP — oral stimulation, taste experiences, and oral hygiene activities may be appropriate
rationale: PEG tube oral activities: require physician/SLP clearance. May include oral stimulation, small taste experiences, oral hygiene, and pleasure feeding (tiny amounts for quality of life) if safe.

stem: A client with chronic pain is learning activity pacing. What does this involve?
options: Breaking activities into manageable segments with scheduled rest breaks BEFORE pain increases, maintaining a consistent activity level throughout the day
Maximum effort followed by days of rest
rationale: Activity pacing: plan rest breaks BEFORE pain increases, maintain consistent activity level (avoid boom-bust cycle), use timers for activity/rest intervals, and gradually increase activity duration.

stem: An OTA is treating a client who demonstrates visual field cut (homonymous hemianopsia). Which eating strategy is helpful?
options: Place food only on the affected side
Turn the plate or rotate the head to scan for all food, use a plate guard, and have someone indicate when food remains on the plate
Only serve food on one side
rationale: Hemianopsia eating strategies: scan the entire plate (turn plate or head), plate guard (contains food), verbal cues to check both sides, well-arranged plate, and systematic scanning training.

stem: A client being discharged from inpatient rehab asks the OTA for their personal cell phone number for questions after discharge. What is the correct response?
options: Explain professional boundaries and provide the facility phone number and appropriate after-hours resources for questions
rationale: Professional boundaries: do not provide personal contact information. Provide facility contact number, after-hours resources, follow-up appointment information, and clear instructions for reaching the team.

stem: An OTA is treating a client with bilateral carpal tunnel syndrome. What nighttime positioning is recommended?
options: Bilateral wrist splints in neutral position (0-5° extension) during sleep to prevent sustained wrist flexion that increases carpal tunnel pressure
rationale: Bilateral CTS night splints: both wrists in neutral (0-5° extension). Prevents sustained flexion during sleep. Both flexion and extension increase carpal tunnel pressure; neutral minimizes it.

stem: When an OTA is conducting a group therapy session, what is the ideal group size for a task-oriented group?
options: 5-7 members — large enough for social interaction but small enough for individual attention and group cohesion
rationale: Task-oriented group ideal size: 5-7 members. Allows adequate social interaction, individual attention, group cohesion, and manageable dynamics. Adjust based on cognitive level and setting.

stem: A client with a new diagnosis of multiple sclerosis asks the OTA about the long-term prognosis. How should the OTA respond?
options: Explain that prognosis questions should be directed to the neurologist; the OTA can focus on current function, energy conservation, and strategies to maintain independence
rationale: Prognosis discussion: outside OT scope to provide medical prognosis. Redirect to the neurologist. OTA can address current function, energy conservation, adaptive strategies, and quality of life.

stem: An OTA observes a new OTA student struggling with a client interaction. What is the mentoring OTA's role?
options: Provide supportive feedback privately, model appropriate client interaction, and help the student develop clinical reasoning skills in a constructive manner
rationale: OTA student mentoring: private constructive feedback, model appropriate behaviors, facilitate clinical reasoning development, encourage self-reflection, and create a safe learning environment.

stem: A client with a dominant hand amputation is learning to write with the non-dominant hand. What training technique facilitates this?
options: Start with large movements (whiteboard), progress to smaller movements (paper), use lined paper, practice letter formation drills, and allow adequate time for neural adaptation
No training needed — it happens naturally
rationale: Non-dominant handwriting training: large movements first (whiteboard), progress to paper, lined paper for consistency, letter formation drills, weighted pen for stability, and patience (neural adaptation takes weeks).

stem: An OTA is providing a sensory integration intervention for a child who avoids playground equipment. What does the OTA use as a clinical reasoning framework?
options: Ayres Sensory Integration (ASI) theory: the just-right challenge, adaptive response, child-directed activity within a structured environment, and sensory-enriched play
rationale: ASI framework: just-right challenge (not too easy/hard), adaptive response (child successfully meets challenge), child-directed (intrinsic motivation), and sensory-enriched environment (varied input types).

stem: A client with a total knee replacement demonstrates difficulty getting in and out of a car. What technique does the OTA teach?
options: Back up to the car seat, hold the car frame, lower onto the seat while extending the operative leg, then pivot and swing both legs in while maintaining extension
rationale: TKR car transfer: stand with back to the car, hold the door/frame, extend the operative leg, lower to the seat, then pivot and swing legs in. Reverse for exit. Recline seat for comfort.

stem: An OTA is treating a client with a healing skin graft on the dorsal hand. Which precaution applies during hand therapy?
options: Avoid shear forces over the graft site, protect from direct pressure, use gentle AROM only (no PROM until cleared), and monitor graft adherence
rationale: Skin graft precautions: avoid shear forces, protect from direct pressure, gentle AROM only (PROM when cleared), monitor graft adherence and color, and splint as indicated to protect the graft.
